Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd put in about 24 hours a week, but it was scattered and unfocused. I'd sit down with good intentions, put on some lectures or podcasts about the material, but my mind would drift. I completed most of my assignments, but studying for exams was a different story entirely. I just couldn't get myself to care enough to really dig into the material systematically.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble. Deadlines were the only thing that really got me moving. Otherwise, I'd tell myself I'd study later, and later never really came in any organized way. It wasn't about regular times—it was about panic.
Q3: Usually an assignment deadline or a notification that something was due soon. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking about something, and I'd realize I hadn't checked in a while. I rarely logged in just to explore or stay on top of things proactively. It was always reactive, never because I genuinely wanted to be there.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd log in when I absolutely had to, grab what I needed, and leave. Some weeks I'd barely touch it at all. I know some people check it daily like social media, but for me it always felt like a chore I was avoiding.
Q5: The recorded lectures were the most useful because I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things, which made it feel less like studying. The discussion boards were okay when classmates explained things in simpler terms than the professor. But honestly, I didn't use most resources to their full potential.
Q6: I ignored the readings almost entirely. I'd download them with good intentions but never actually read them. The supplementary materials, extra videos, anything labeled 'optional'—I just didn't bother. I told myself I'd get to them, but there was always something else competing for my attention, or I just didn't have the energy.
Q7: If it was graded, it felt worth my time. That's the honest truth. If I could see a direct connection between doing something and passing the course, I'd do it. Anything that felt abstract or like it was just 'enrichment' got pushed aside. I needed immediate, tangible stakes to motivate me.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable. Required ones I'd force myself through, but optional? My brain just categorized them as unnecessary. There's something about knowing something won't hurt your grade that makes it easy to ignore, even if it might actually help you learn.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download audio versions of lectures or convert materials so I could listen while commuting or doing chores. I also talked through concepts with a study group sometimes—that peer support helped more than I expected. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was real studying for me.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours discussing course material with friends over Discord or in person, working through problems together. Or I'd be listening to lectures on my phone while walking around. The LMS would show I logged in once, grabbed a file, and left—but I actually engaged with that content for much longer offline.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them multiple times, but the LMS only registered that initial download. Same with PDFs—I'd open them once online, save them, and then study from my laptop without being connected. My offline engagement was way higher than my login data suggests.
Q12: Around midterms, my anxiety really spiked and I just couldn't focus. I'd sit down to study and my mind would race with everything that could go wrong. The material started feeling overwhelming, and I didn't have the resilience to push through. I'd end up just doing the bare minimum to submit assignments.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had multiple deadlines across courses, this one always got deprioritized because I felt like I was already behind. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate on lectures—I'd zone out even while listening. The confusion built up because I wasn't attending classes, so I'd feel lost and just disengage further.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other things or just feeling numb. I was technically 'studying' but nothing was sticking. It was more about going through the motions than actually learning.
Q15: All the time. I knew exam prep was crucial but I kept putting it off because the anxiety of facing how much I didn't know was paralyzing. It was easier to complete assignments where I could just follow instructions than to actually study and confront the gaps in my understanding. Avoidance became my default.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS consistently. I'd see a due date approaching and finally log in, rush through whatever needed to be done. Without deadlines, I'd drift for weeks. They created artificial urgency that my brain apparently needed to function.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, but the effect wore off quickly. Good feedback made me feel okay about coasting; critical feedback made me anxious and want to avoid the course even more. It was a mixed bag—neither type really changed my overall engagement pattern.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but was actually bad for someone like me. I needed more external pressure and I didn't get it. The course assumed students would self-regulate, but I'm terrible at that. More mandatory check-ins might have kept me on track.
Q19: The lack of mandatory attendance made it too easy to skip. I'd tell myself I'd watch the recordings later, but 'later' accumulated into a mountain of unwatched content. Having everything available asynchronously should help, but for me it just enabled my avoidance. More structure would have forced engagement.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. I'd log in frequently to check things, download stuff, maybe start watching videos—but then not follow through on the actual learning. High activity can just mean someone is anxious and checking constantly without actually doing the deep work. The LMS tracks clicks, not comprehension.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies mostly offline, uses textbooks, works with peers outside the platform—they'd look disengaged online but actually be learning effectively. That wasn't quite my experience since I failed, but I know my peer interactions and offline listening didn't show up at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long you stay. But they miss everything qualitative. They can't tell if you're actually thinking while watching a video or just letting it play. They miss offline study, peer conversations, and whether someone is anxious or checked out while technically 'engaged.'
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just completing tasks to pass. It means being curious, asking questions, connecting ideas. For me, I was rarely truly engaged—I was just surviving, doing what I had to do to submit assignments. Real engagement requires mental presence I often lacked.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels or how much time I spent paralyzed by worry instead of studying. It can't capture the peer support conversations that actually helped me understand concepts, or the hours of audio lectures I listened to offline. It definitely can't show that I cared about doing well but couldn't translate that into effective action.
